statement: A practical prior for isotropic P-wave speed in crystalline upper continental crust is centered near 6.0 km/s, with a few tenths of km/s scatter reflecting common compositional and regional variation.
source: Christensen and Mooney continental-crust seismic velocity compilation; CRUST1.0 reference crustal model

statement: For crystalline upper continental crust, S-wave velocity is commonly near 3.4 to 3.6 km/s, so a Normal prior with mean 3.45 km/s and moderate spread is suitable for generic crustal propagation models.
source: Christensen and Mooney continental-crust seismic velocity compilation; Shearer, Introduction to Seismology

statement: The uppermost-mantle Pn velocity beneath typical continental lithosphere is usually close to 8.1 km/s, consistent with global 1-D travel-time reference models.
source: IASP91 and ak135 global 1-D seismic travel-time reference models

statement: A generic prior for the continental-crust Vp/Vs ratio is centered near 1.73, close to the common quartzofeldspathic-crust value, with enough spread for mafic, felsic, fractured, or fluid-affected crust.
source: Christensen and Mooney continental-crust velocity compilation; Aki and Richards, Quantitative Seismology

statement: Fundamental-mode Rayleigh-wave phase velocity at about 20 s period over continental crust and uppermost mantle is often around 3.5 km/s, with regional dispersion and lithospheric structure motivating a moderately broad prior.
source: Shearer, Introduction to Seismology; PREM surface-wave dispersion reference examples

statement: The bulk density of crystalline continental crust is commonly near 2800 kg/m3, with roughly 100 to 200 kg/m3 variation across felsic-to-mafic crustal compositions and depth averages.
source: CRUST1.0 global crustal model; Christensen and Mooney continental-crust compilation

statement: A standard prior for uppermost-mantle density is centered near 3350 kg/m3, appropriate for peridotitic mantle just below the Moho in global reference Earth models.
source: Dziewonski and Anderson Preliminary Reference Earth Model (PREM)

statement: For average crystalline crust at local-to-regional seismic frequencies, P-wave quality factor Qp is positive and strongly variable; this LogNormal prior uses natural-log parameters with median about 600.
source: Aki and Richards, Quantitative Seismology; Shearer, Introduction to Seismology

statement: S-wave quality factor in average continental crust is commonly lower than Qp and varies substantially by tectonic region; this LogNormal prior uses natural-log parameters with median about 300.
source: Aki and Richards, Quantitative Seismology; Sato, Fehler and Maeda, Seismic Wave Propagation and Scattering in the Heterogeneous Earth

statement: For 1 Hz S waves in average crystalline crust, the amplitude attenuation coefficient in an exponential path term exp(-alpha r) is often on the order of 0.003 km^-1, corresponding to Qs of a few hundred and Vs near 3.5 km/s; the LogNormal parameters are in natural-log space.
source: Aki and Richards, Quantitative Seismology, using the standard alpha = pi f / (Q v) attenuation relation

statement: The near-site high-frequency spectral decay parameter kappa0 for hard-rock to generic rock sites is nonnegative and commonly a few hundredths of a second; a HalfNormal prior with scale 0.04 s gives that order of magnitude while allowing larger attenuating sites.
source: Anderson and Hough kappa high-frequency spectral-decay model

statement: For crustal attenuation laws of the form Q(f) = Q0 f^eta over local-to-regional seismic bands, eta is usually between 0 and 1 and often near 0.5 to 0.7; this Beta prior has mean 0.6.
source: Sato, Fehler and Maeda, Seismic Wave Propagation and Scattering in the Heterogeneous Earth; standard coda-Q empirical summaries
